name: FHIR ContextObjects - Vitals Window
summary: Turn high-frequency vitals streams (HR, PULSE, RESP, SpO2) into time-windowed ContextObjects where each node carries a standard FHIR Observation payload.
description: This strategy models wearable/monitor numeric signals as one ContextObject per patient + device + time window. Inside the ContextObject, each measurement becomes a node whose data is a FHIR Observation. The result is a consistent unit of storage and retrieval that remains FHIR-aligned and is easy to query and enrich in MongoDB.

bulk.description: Bulk operation settings (CLI/API only, not visible in portal)

ui.story:
Wearable and monitor data arrives as continuous numeric streams. Storing every point as an isolated record makes it hard to retrieve meaningful context.

This strategy uses a simple pattern:
- Build one ContextObject per patient + device + time window
- Store measurements as nodes inside that ContextObject
- Keep each node payload as a standard FHIR Observation

Applications then read one document to obtain a coherent window of vitals, ready for dashboards, alerts, summarization, and enrichment.
ui.how_it_works:
Input: CSV numeric streams with time and metrics (HR, PULSE, RESP, SpO2).

Steps:
1) Normalize timestamps and bucket rows into fixed windows (e.g., 60s, 300s, 900s)
2) For each window, emit one ContextObject with subjects and an interval (start/end)
3) Emit nodes[] for measurements, each node containing:
   - p: tokenized path (e.g., FHIR.Observation|<code>|SpO2)
   - t: effective time (ISO timestamp)
   - data: full FHIR Observation JSON

Output: ContextObjects stored in MongoDB as the unit of record.
benefits: FHIR-aligned payloads: each measurement is a FHIR Observation
Predictable retrieval: one document gives you a full time window
Extensible: add derived metrics, quality flags, or embeddings later without changing the contract
Practical: simple model that works well for operational use cases
ui.ideal_for: Best when you need operational access to vitals streams for follow-up programs (e.g., cancer monitoring), alerting, trending, cohort filters, and AI summarization, while preserving FHIR semantics.
ui.consider_alternatives: If you only need batch analytics, a lakehouse/time-series-only approach may be enough. If you need point-by-point alerting under very tight latency, use streaming evaluation plus this strategy as the durable operational record.

primary.title: BIDMC PPG and Respiration Dataset (PhysioNet)
primary.note: Used as sample numeric streams (HR, PULSE, RESP, SpO2) for windowed ContextObject generation.

cards.title: Unit of record
cards.body: One ContextObject per patient + device + time window.

cards.title: Node payload
cards.body: Each node stores a FHIR Observation JSON payload.

cards.title: Transform
cards.body: CSV rows → window bucketing → Observation nodes → ContextObject envelope.

ops.name: preview_windows
ops.summary: Preview window segmentation and node counts without writing to MongoDB
